questions.question: What problem usually pushes a system from plain queues toward a workflow engine?
options: The business process needs durable long-running state, timers, callbacks, and visible progress.
The service needs to resize one image after upload.
The team wants to avoid storing any status in a database.
The product no longer cares about failures.
questions.explanation: Workflow engines exist for stateful orchestration, not just async execution.

questions.question: Why are timers first-class in workflow design?
options: Because waits, deadlines, backoff, and escalation often matter as much as step execution.
Because timers guarantee external side effects are reversible.
Because queues do not allow retries.
Because timers remove the need for activity workers.
questions.explanation: Long-running workflows are often dominated by waiting and timeout behavior.

questions.question: Which answer sounds weakest in a workflow interview?
options: We will expose workflow status from durable state and define compensation for partial failure.
We will model callbacks and approvals as signals into a workflow instance.
We will keep the state scattered across retries, cron jobs, and operator intuition because the queue already tracks attempts.
We will separate deterministic orchestration from side-effecting activities.
questions.explanation: Hidden orchestration state is exactly what workflow engines are meant to avoid.

questions.question: What metric most directly shows workflows are getting stuck?
options: Oldest active workflow age or oldest step age by workflow type.
Only producer host disk size.
The number of headings in the runbook.
Average commit message length.
questions.explanation: Aging workflow state reveals stuck progress better than total counts alone.

questions.question: Why is compensation different from ordinary retry?
options: Compensation handles a later-step failure after earlier side effects already happened and must be unwound or reconciled.
Compensation is just a second immediate retry of the same request.
Compensation removes the need for status APIs.
Compensation is only for database replication lag.
questions.explanation: Compensation is about reversing or reconciling partial progress, not just reattempting work.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with workflow engines?
options: You gain durable orchestration and debuggable status, but you pay with more metadata, platform complexity, and step modeling effort.
You gain exactly-once side effects across all external providers for free.
You remove the need for idempotent activities.
You make short-lived stateless jobs cheaper than queues.
questions.explanation: Workflow engines are powerful but operationally heavier.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of using cron and tables as a substitute for workflow state?
options: The real orchestration logic becomes implicit, brittle, and difficult to debug under partial failures.
The system automatically preserves all compensation rules.
The queue can no longer redeliver messages.
The product loses the ability to send emails.
questions.explanation: Implicit orchestration tends to fragment ownership and observability.

questions.question: Which question matters most before committing to a workflow engine?
options: Whether the process truly has long-running state, timers, and externally visible progress worth modeling explicitly.
Whether the team prefers blue dashboards to green ones.
Whether payloads are compressed with gzip.
Whether every task can fit into one partition.
questions.explanation: That question determines whether the orchestration overhead is justified.
